data.title: Madison Conservative Allocation Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Recap – Third Quarter 2024

After a rough start, U.S. stocks bounced back to generate another impressive gain, with the S&P 500 Index up 5.9% for the third quarter of 2024, pushing the year-to-date total return to a staggering 22.1%, not far off 2023’s outsized 26.3% return, with three months left to go in the year.

If you hadn’t heard the term “yen carry trade” before, it was likely burned into your lexicon by early August. The rapid ascent of the formerly shunned Japanese currency was behind a nasty bout of volatility that took U.S. stocks down nearly 10%, as investors were forced to quickly unwind levered investments and prevent further losses as the yen moved against them. Behind the yen’s rise was a stark contrast in the outlook for monetary policy between the U.S. and Japan, which came to a head in the final week of July when Federal Reserve Chair Powell leaned dovish while the Bank of Japan hawkishly raised interest rates, then a sour U.S. employment report added fuel to the rate cut fire. However, the Bank of Japan yielded via softer comments around future policy and the markets went back to rallying on the belief of much easier monetary policy (lower interest rates) moving forward in the U.S.

The shifting market sentiment towards the anticipation of even lower rates propelled cyclically-based and smaller cap stocks to big gains, especially early in the quarter.

Technology stocks were notable underperformers and the mixed performance of the so-called Magnificent 7, along with other mega cap stocks led to a broader and seemingly healthier market advance. Overall, the small cap Russell 2000 Index jumped 9.3% versus the S&P 500’s 5.9% return, and the more economically sensitive Russell 1000 Value Index gained 9.4%, well above the 3.2% return for the technology-heavy Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Big gains were not just for stocks. Bond returns were boosted by a steep drop in interest rates across the yield curve from the increased expectations of deeper Fed rate cuts. Entering the quarter, investors expected the Fed Funds rate to fall just short of 0.5% by year end, and largely expected the Fed to cut by the standard 0.25% for their highly anticipated first rate cut of the cycle on September 18th. However, belief in something bigger built after Chair Powell’s comment that “the time has come for policy to adjust” during his remarks at Jackson Hole in August, as he detailed his growing confidence that inflation is under control and that the Fed is now more concerned about the cooling labor market. As the September Fed meeting approached, market odds firmed around a larger 0.5% cut, and on that fateful day, the Fed delivered with a jumbo half-point cut to kick off the cutting cycle and guided for another 0.5% of cuts to come by January. However, market optimism ran higher, exiting the quarter with an implied rate 0.75% below today’s level, with dreams of a 3% Fed Funds rate by the end of 2025. The falling interest rate hoopla drove the broad Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index to a near S&P 500 matching 5.2% return for the quarter, bringing the nine-month total up to 4.5%.

Outside of the U.S., China pushed the Bank of Japan out of the spotlight by launching a massive stimulus plan during the final week of the quarter. Investors welcomed Beijing’s willingness to turn the monetary spigot wide open by bidding Chinese equity indexes up over 20% in seven days. The upward explosion of Chinese stocks helped boost international stocks and the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index to an 8.1% return, bettering the U.S. market for the quarter.

Performance

The broadening of the market beyond the small set of very large U.S. companies benefited portfolios for the quarter. The returns of value and smaller stocks helped several of our U.S. equity positions generate outperformance to balance out the underperformance of our energy and technology allocations. Results from our international equity positioning were aided by our positions in emerging Asia but restrained by our overweight stance on Japanese and higher quality developed markets stocks. Our fixed income allocations performed roughly inline with the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index, aided by strong returns from our holdings in mortgage-backed and longer duration positions.

Our cautious stance on equity exposure, which we detail in the next section, was the largest detractor to performance.

Outlook & Positioning

Our primary rationale for remaining cautious in our asset allocation positioning hinges on the dramatic difference in market expectations and pricing between the bond and commodities markets compared to the stock market.

Starting with the bond market, as of the end of the third quarter, Fed Funds futures are pricing in nearly 2% of additional rate cuts over the next 12 months, which would bring the Fed Funds rate to around 3%. Cutting rates at that speed and magnitude has historically only occurred in a recessionary environment. The price of oil declined by 20% during the quarter, surveys of manufacturing activity remain contractionary, the index of leading economic indicators has not recorded a positive reading since February 2022, a month before the Fed began to tighten monetary policy. These are not typical signposts of a healthy economy. On the other hand, the S&P 500 charges on to repeated all-time highs, with an atypically-high valuation level and an expectation for nearly 15% earnings growth for 2025. Whatever a “soft landing” might be, we don’t believe this is it. If the bond market is correct, we are headed for an economic reckoning in the near future and stocks are due for a rude awakening. If the stock market is correct, the economic expansion is set to continue without needing a landing. In that case, it’s highly unlikely that we’ll see 2% of rate cuts over the next year and longer-term rates have fallen too far.

What’s clear is that one market has it wrong. The resolution of this condition will likely be quite volatile. We believe the bond market appears closer to fair value than stocks. As such, we remain moderately underweight equities given the highly uncertain and contradictory environment.

We truly appreciate your confidence and partnership.

data.title: Madison High Quality Bond Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Economic data during the third quarter of 2024 lent support to the Fed’s case for declining inflationary pressures and stable economic activity. With evidence of continued progress towards their stated goals, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) acted to reduce the Fed Funds rate at their September 18th meeting. This rate reduction initiated the long-awaited policy easing hinted at by Chairman Powell just over a year ago when the FOMC ‘pivoted’ policy direction.

Maintaining The 'Just Right' Landing

The much-debated economic ‘soft landing’ appears to be turning into a ‘just right’ landing with gradually slowing inflation and stable economic growth. The Fed’s preferred inflation measure, Core PCE, slowed to 2.7% year-over-year, showing meaningful progress towards their stated 2% target. Meanwhile, second quarter final GDP showed the economy grew at an annualized rate of 3% while expectations for the third quarter remain near 2.5%. Meanwhile, labor market concerns emerged as initial claims and hiring data suggested a more rapid decline in recent months. The 4-week moving average of initial weekly jobless claims, now above 225k, remains elevated and well above the sub-200k level seen earlier this year. Additionally, the unemployment rate has risen to 4.2%, the highest level since 2022. The string of weaker labor market data drew the attention of the FOMC heading into the September meeting as several members highlighted the need to adjust policy proactively to avoid more severe labor market weakness. The recent shift to better inflation data, following higher than expected inflation to start the year, has been welcomed by Fed members and markets alike. However, weakening labor market data could push the Fed to increase policy easing to avoid a ‘hard landing’.

Higher For Longer Comes To An End

With the FOMC gaining confidence that progress was continuing towards their stated 2% inflation goal, at the September 18th FOMC meeting the committee refocused upon the labor market and weakening employment data. At that September meeting, the Fed cut its interest rate target range 50 basis points from 5.25-5.50% to 4.75-5.00%. Along with the interest rate target cut, the Fed’s median interest rate forecast was also reduced to 4.375% by year-end 2024 and to 3.375% by year-end 2025. This is likely a function of inflation slowing consistently over the past six months giving the FOMC increased confidence that their targeted 2% inflation level was attainable. With increased confidence they were on the right track, they were able to shift focus towards reducing restrictive policy toward preventing a material economic slowdown.

As investors evaluated benign economic data, expectations for a rate cut at the September FOMC policy meeting grew stronger. By the time the meeting date arrived, the Fed’s move was widely anticipated with investors pushing U.S. Treasury (“Treasury”) yields sharply lower. During the quarter, two-year Treasury yields declined 111 basis points while ten-year Treasury yields fell 62 basis points. These changes led to a sharply steeper yield curve and ended the ‘inverted’ two-year to ten-year Treasury curve in place since June 2022. Market expectations for future rate cuts are currently priced slightly more aggressively than the FOMC’s median rate targets, suggesting investors are concerned about a more severe economic slow-down and the need for aggressive rate cuts. While the direction of policy is no longer in question, the exact path for policy remains dependent upon fundamental data. The FOMC remains ‘data dependent’ and has shown a willingness to be patient and methodical in their approach. We expect continued rate cuts and view the aggressive initial 50 basis-point rate cut as an attempt to get ahead of softer economic data. Going forward, we expect continued rate volatility as each economic release, especially employment and inflation data, is evaluated for its potential impact on future FOMC policy actions.

Spreads Discounting ‘Ideal’ Outcome

For the quarter, intermediate corporate bond spreads tightened five basis points, with Utilities, Financials, and single-A corporate bonds tightening the most. Year-to-date, spreads were eleven basis points tighter, with Financial spreads 22 basis points tighter, Utility spreads nine basis points tighter, and Industrial spreads unchanged. Financial corporate bonds continue to offer more attractive spreads than higher-quality Industrial corporate bonds. However, given the recent outperformance of Financial corporate bonds, even Financial spreads are now tighter than historical levels. Given tighter spreads and lower interest rates, corporate bonds have experienced positive total returns in 2024. The Bloomberg Intermediate Corporate index posted a +4.66% total return in the third quarter and a +5.71% total return year-to-date. However, with spreads near the tightest levels over the last decade, they provide little cushion if the economy weakens meaningfully.

Performance & Positioning

During the third quarter, the Madison High Quality Bond Fund (Class Y) underperformed the benchmark, returning +3.79% versus the Bloomberg Intermediate Government Credit A+ Index return of +4.07%. Sector/ Quality, Yield/Income, and Yield Curve positioning proved additive to relative performance as credit spreads tightened, portfolio yield exceeded the benchmark, and the yield curve steepened during the period. Duration positioning was detractive to relative performance as rates declined sharply during the quarter. Security selection was slightly detractive to performance as high-quality industrial sectors underperformed financials during the period.

In recent months, the debate has evolved from whether the Fed has achieved a ‘soft landing’ to how to maintain it. The Fed, far from proclaiming a ‘mission accomplished’ moment, has shifted their focus to avoiding labor market deterioration. This new focus was clear during the September FOMC meeting and the resulting 50 basis point cut. While their direction is now clear, the path forward is far from obvious. Continued data volatility and changing market expectations will drive markets until a clear policy end point emerges. The challenge to continue reducing inflation without materially slowing the economy will dictate policy changes over the coming months. We are pleased to see fixed income portfolios benefitting as yields decline, but current yield levels, while still offering value, are less attractive than earlier this year. We are pleased to have increased duration exposure and yield in portfolio’s while we evaluate future Fed policy. We expect further yield movement will be dictated by changing economic fundamentals and stand ready to take further action as expectations for monetary policy adjust in the months ahead.

Disclosures

The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. data.title: CGBL: A Flexible 60/40 Fund From Capital Group
data.description:
Thesis

The 60/40 portfolio is the classic formula for retail investors who take a more passive approach to portfolio construction. The largest name in the space is the iShares Core Growth Allocation ETF (AOR), which comes with a $2 billion AUM and a very recognized brand. AOR, however, is fixed in its sectoral allocation, while some investors prefer to run their own 60/40 portfolios in order to get more flexibility.

Capital Group Core Balanced ETF (NYSEARCA:CGBL) is an exchange-traded fund that broadly follows the 60/40 allocation but with active management and flexibility in its allocations:

With a portfolio of quality stocks and exposure to bonds, this balanced fund invests between 50% and 75% in equities, with flexible exposure to growth-oriented and dividend-paying stocks. The fixed income portion is invested in one or more Capital Group fixed income ETFs, seeking to provide diversification from equities and income.

In essence, the fund adjusts its equity and fixed income positions as it sees fit per the macrocycle, and furthermore takes an active approach in selecting the individual equities which compose its portfolio.

In this article, we are going to take a closer look at CGBL, look at its composition and structure, and articulate why we believe this ETF is a better choice than an outright AOR allocation.

Fund composition

As we have seen from the fund definition, the vehicle aims for a flexible 60/40 composition, at the portfolio manager's discretion. Currently, the name is overweight equities:

Equities currently represent over 66% of the portfolio, with U.S. equities representing the largest majority. Bonds come up to roughly 32% of the portfolio, with the rest being represented by cash.

Equity Slice - not what you would expect

A staple of this fund is the active approach to portfolio construction. When looking at the equity component here, what strikes us is the active allocation approach that looks different than a plain S&P 500 allocation:

Firstly, please note the above percentages are expressed as a ratio of net assets (i.e. bonds included). Secondly, please note the glaring absence from the 'Top Equities' table of some of the Magnificent 7 names which the market follows closely currently. Whereas AOR utilizes other ETFs as building blocks for its 60% equities allocation, this name takes a more prescriptive approach and actually selects individual names.

Furthermore, by taking an underweight approach for the tech sector, the ETF is able to construct a portfolio with a more appealing valuation profile when compared to the S&P 500 index:

The fund has a price to book ratio of only 3.5x versus a 4.3x one for a plain vanilla 60/40 blend, a price to cash flow of only 13.7x versus 15.6x for the benchmark, and most important, an attractive blended P/E ratio of 18.9x versus 21.6x for the benchmark.

Market pundits have raised significant valuation concerns for the S&P 500 index, with the levels at historic highs. The index has to deliver significant earnings growth in order to fulfill its lofty expectations.

Bond Slice - robust build with rates risk

The bond slice in the fund is concentrated in treasuries and MBS securities rather than credit risky corporate bonds:

U.S. Treasuries and AAA MBS bonds represent over 53% of the portfolio, with the residual mostly in high grade corporate bonds. We can see from the above table that low-rated BB and B bonds are only 10% of net assets.

This build translates into a bond sleeve which is mostly driven by rates and its duration. The fund comes with an intermediate duration of 6 years and a 4.9% average yield to worst. This is a classic but robust build, which ensures a balance between bonds and equities in a negative correlation macro set-up:

A negative return correlation means that, on average, when equity returns decrease, bond returns increase, and vice-versa. This inverse relationship between equity and bond returns has helped multi-asset portfolios weather various economic and market downturns.

The beauty of a 60/40 portfolio (whether static or flexible) is that it exhibits a lower standard deviation than a stock portfolio via the benefits of negative correlation. When equities are down significantly, market participants flock to treasury bonds for safety, thus putting a positive NAV performance in for the bond slice.

Performance versus benchmark

The fund was launched in 2023; thus its performance history is limited. However, the name has managed to outperform the static AOR in the past year:

The two names have a very close historic correlation, with a recent outperformance for CGBL. The outperformance can be explained by the flexible bond sleeve, with long bonds having been pressured since September, thus underperforming. CGBL shows that an active approach to the classic 60/40 portfolio is beneficial, and while the fund does not have to re-invent the wheel, small tweaks in times of monetary tightening or loosening can make a significant difference.

We expect this trend to continue. While the outperformance will not be eye-popping, it will nonetheless be very significant when looking at total return on a relative value basis. 500 bps for a classic 60/40 portfolio is very significant, and over time similar levels result in diverging annual total returns.

Why we like CGBL

We like CGBL because of its active management to the classic 60/40 portfolio. The past two years have clearly shown us that rates moves are not linear, and the latest yield widening in the past two months are a testament to that. Long rates as expressed via the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) are down -7% since mid-September 2024, while intermediate rates as expressed by the US Treasury 10 Year Note ETF (UTEN) are down -3.6% in the same time-frame.

Having the flexibility to navigate the non-linear rates environment is a key to success in our mind, while in a broader macro set up, the fund can outperform even more when correctly identifying macro tightening or easing cycles.

The second aspect which is appealing in respect to this name is the active approach to equity selection, which tends to focus on lower P/E names. As valuations get even more stretched, this approach holds tremendous value.

Downside risk factors

The main risk for this name is a re-acceleration in inflation, which would result in a recession and wider bond spreads. The ETF works best in a negative correlation environment between equities and bonds. If both tank as a result of another inflationary spike, we are going to see a scenario similar to 2022, when both equities and bonds lost value.

While the bond sleeve has an intermediate duration profile, it is nonetheless subject to wider rates. Similarly, for the equity sleeve - even though the current valuation as reflected in its P/E ratio is acceptable, there is significant downside to be had in case of a recession.

A middle of the road recession on the back of a weak consumer would affect CGBL to a lesser extent given the long bonds sleeve will post a positive performance, partly compensating for the losses in the equity allocation.

Conclusion

CGBL is an exchange-traded fund. The name is a flexible take on a classic 60/40 portfolio, and represents a better choice to allocate to such strategy when compared with AOR. The fund currently has a 66% allocation to equities and 32% in bonds, with the rest in cash. The name has a 50% to 75% allocation band for equities, that is at the manager's discretion. The bond sleeve comes with a 6-year duration, and is overweight treasuries and MBS bonds. CGBL has managed to outperform AOR in the past year by roughly 500 bps, and we are of the opinion that it will continue to do so via its allocation flexibility.

data.title: JEPI: A Stellar Buying Opportunity As Volatility Trends Up
data.description:
JEPI's Solid Risk-Adjusted Strategy

Since my last update in July 2024, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) investors have outperformed the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) on a total return basis. The period of outperformance coincided with marked volatility in August and September 2024, spurred by the reversal of the yen carry trade and worries about a potential recession.

However, the Fed's aggressive 50 bps interest rate reduction has helped lower the risks of a weakening labor market, corroborating the central bank's resolve to avoid a hard landing. Recent labor market indicators and resilient consumer spending have bolstered a seasonally strong Q4 as investors brush aside the bearish prognostications.

As we draw closer to the US presidential election in early November 2024, the S&P VIX index (VIX) could consolidate and potentially move higher as investors start hedging unanticipated risks. Since President Biden's withdrawal from the election, the race between Donald Trump and Kamala Harris is getting increasingly close, with no clear winner in sight. Notwithstanding the market's bullishness, as we head into the final two months of 2024, a potentially higher VIX could benefit JEPI investors.

In my previous bullish JEPI article, I highlighted why the fund's construct suits income investors seeking equity market appreciation. It's exposed to fundamentally strong companies listed in the S&P 500, underpinning the ETF's strategy. Therefore, the ETF's relative outperformance didn't surprise me. However, on a 1Y total return basis, JEPI's return of 21% has significantly underperformed the S&P 500's 38% gain over the same period. Hence, some investors might point out that the ETF's covered call options sleeve limited the fund's potential, notwithstanding JEPI's attractive dividend yield (trailing twelve months) of more than 7%.

Investors must consider JEPI's superior risk-adjusted returns against its category and index peers. Therefore, assessing the ETF solely on its total return may not be the most appropriate yardstick, given its strategy of gaining market exposure while offering potentially reduced volatility. Therefore, I assess that the ETF's defensive construct should still appeal to more defensive income investors looking for a relatively attractive monthly dividend payout.

JEPI: Equity Exposure Helps Provide Upside Bias

Furthermore, a more dovish Fed through 2025 should also underpin the ETF's appeal to income investors seeking to reallocate their exposure from cash. While a continued bull market rally could lead to a more significant performance bifurcation with the S&P 500, investors mustn't throw caution to the wind as we enter a potentially volatile election period. Wall Street's estimates on the S&P 500 are increasingly bullish, projecting a 15% increase in operating EPS for 2025 following 2024's estimated 9.5% growth.

As a reminder, JEPI's fund managers aim to allocate “at least 80% of its Assets in equity securities.” While the covered-call strategy aims to lower the fund's volatility risks, investors can still benefit from the fund's pro-equities construct, as the ETF seeks to capture the market's upside bias.

In addition, JEPI's focus on technology (XLK) stocks aligns well with the secular growth thesis of the AI growth inflection. I discussed the robust growth prospects of the AI value chain in a recent Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) article. While JEPI's broad diversification aims to reduce the concentration risks in individual stock holdings, the focus on the tech sector is assessed as appropriate.

Furthermore, the ETF benefits from investing in economically sensitive sectors such as Industrials (XLI) and Financials (XLF), as the recessionary thesis increasingly loses favor with investors. Hence, unless we fall into an unanticipated hard landing (not my base case), I believe the market dynamics are expected to favor JEPI's investment strategy.

Is JEPI ETF A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

VIX's price action suggests we could be moving into a phase of potentially higher volatility as investors hedge their risks before the US presidential election. However, the sharp moves seen in August aren't expected, as the economy isn't anticipated to fall into a hard landing.

Therefore, the ETF's pro-cyclical focus and secular tech sector exposure are apt as the economy continues to perform resiliently. In addition, barring significant downside volatility, short-term pullbacks could offer JEPI investors the opportunity to experience an improvement in its covered call strategy. Hence, I assess the market environment as increasingly constructive for JEPI as we inch closer to November.

As seen above, JEPI's medium-term uptrend (adjusted for dividends) has remained incredibly robust since its bottom in October 2023. The ETF then rose through March 2024 and consolidated for almost six months before it bottomed in August 2024. It then benefited from the market's recovery, riding the rally while lowering volatility through its covered call strategy.

Based on my assessment, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF could be due for a short-term pullback, as it has surged into well-overbought zones. Despite that, I assess that its attractive dividend yields and apt sector exposure should continue to attract defensive investors seeking a blend of equity appreciation and income investing.

Notwithstanding the benefits of JEPI's strategy, investors must be aware that its covered call strategy limits upside potential, as observed by its relative underperformance to the S&P 500. Even though it may outperform on a risk-adjusted basis, it may not be appropriate for more aggressive equity investors who aren't concerned with short-term downside volatility.

In addition, JEPI's covered call strategy cannot fully absorb downside volatility if the moves exceed the options premium from its ELN out-of-the-money call options writing. Given its equity exposure, the fund could face significant downside volatility, as seen in its price chart. Therefore, it's not suitable for highly defensive investors who cannot tolerate the potential downside attributed to its equity investments.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Important note: Investors are reminded to do their due diligence and not rely on the information provided as financial advice. Consider this article as supplementing your required research. Please always apply independent thinking. Note that the rating is not intended to time a specific entry/exit at the point of writing unless otherwise specified.

data.title: 7 Steps To Create A New High-Dividend Index
data.description:
By Kathleen Dobyns, Senior Manager - Product Management

If they are reliable payers, high-dividend stocks can help investors in two ways: they boost the income from an equity portfolio while offering potential capital appreciation.

Building a high-income equity portfolio requires sophistication

Building a successful high-dividend portfolio requires some sophistication. The same is true for the designer of a high-dividend index.

On the one hand, stocks with higher dividend yields are often mature companies with established cash flows and a history of consistent dividend payments, which shows a commitment to returning cash to shareholders.

However, a high yield could also signal a lower-than-average prospective earnings growth rate (which, in turn, could limit the potential for share price appreciation).

High-income stocks can provide investors with a degree of stability in volatile market conditions, but a naïve stock selection approach - choosing companies with the highest historical dividend yields - would probably leave the investor exposed.

This is because an abnormally high dividend yield (often referred to as a “yield trap”) is often a precursor of a dividend cut: traders usually anticipate such cuts by marking down share prices, and dividend yields are calculated using backward-looking data.

To meet growing investor interest, FTSE Russell launched the FTSE Global Equity High Income index series in May 2024. The index series uses a simple, capitalisation-weighted methodology to reflect the performance of global stocks with relatively high tax-adjusted dividend yields, while also addressing concerns over such possible yield traps.

Seven construction steps

The index series follows seven intuitive design steps, as outlined below:

  • High-dividend indices vary in the complexity of their construction methodologies. For this index series, we’ve chosen a simple rank-and-select rule to choose constituents.
  • We use forecast dividends, rather than trailing (historical) dividends to rank potential index constituents; although there is a close alignment between the two measures and past dividends are often a reliable indicator of future payments, forecast dividends cover almost all the global equity markets and are more responsive to changes in market conditions.

Forecast dividend coverage by cumulative index constituent weights

  • We rank potential index constituents by net-of-tax yield, recognising the fact that index users often receive dividends after a deduction for withholding tax.
  • To reduce the risk of yield traps, we exclude stocks with zero forecast or trailing dividends and those in the bottom fifth percentile of companies with negative one-year returns. This step screens out some of the stocks most at risk of cutting dividends.
  • To enable easier country or regional carve-outs of the index series, we rank stocks within each of the eight regional universes for the FTSE Global Equity Index Series (Asia Pacific ex China ex Japan, China, Japan, Developed Europe, Emerging Europe, Middle East & Africa, Latin America and North America).
  • To ensure relatively low concentration risk, to maintain diversification and to aid investability, we select the top 50% of securities by dividend yield within each regional universe and weight them by market capitalisation.
  • To constrain turnover while ensuring regular index maintenance, we incorporate buffer rules and rebalance the index annually, with the quarterly removal of securities with zero forecast or trailing dividends.

A near-doubling of the index dividend yield

Applying these construction steps to the FTSE All-World index (via a back-test) resulted in a consistent near-doubling of the index’s dividend yield between 2002 and 2024 - see the chart.

FTSE All-World and FTSE All-World Equity High Income index dividend yields (%)

As equity dividend yields are on the decline, this could be a valuable set of selection rules for income-seeking investors - and the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index yield is calculated net of tax. But what about the effect on returns and risk?

Return and risk statistics

The total returns of the FTSE All-World Equity High Income and the FTSE All-World indices were broadly similar for most of the 22-year period, with the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index returning 9.07% per annum between September 2022 and September 2024 and the FTSE All-World index returning 10.14% per annum over the period.

In fact, the two indices’ total return ratio stayed close to 100 between 2002 and 2017, after which the outperformance of select low-dividend or zero-dividend stocks, particularly in the US equity market’s tech sector, helped the FTSE All-World index to pull ahead.

However, the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index had lower annualised volatility than the FTSE All-World index, confirming the observation that higher-dividend stocks are often stable businesses with higher cash flows and are somewhat less risky than the market.

Total return comparison: FTSE All-World Equity High Income index/FTSE All-World index

Total return p.a. (2002-2024) Standard deviation p.a. (2002-2024)
FTSE All-World Index 10.14% 15.49%
FTSE All-World Equity High Income Index 9.07% 14.75%

Source: FTSE Russell, data from 30/9/2002-30/9/2024, 30/9/2002=100. All performance presented for the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index prior to the index inception date is back-tested performance. Past performance is not a guide to future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

Further information for equity income seekers

As global interest rates head lower, investors are seeking sources of portfolio income. The FTSE Global Equity High Income index series is a comprehensive market capitalisation-weighted benchmark representing global stocks that pay relatively higher dividend yields.

data.title: Globant Is A Great Business, But There's Not Enough Value To Buy
data.description:
Globant (NYSE:GLOB) stock is not cheap, and I expect that its valuation multiples will contract slightly in the next 12 months. That said, I admire the company's clever operating model (including nearshore development) and strong positioning amid high digital transformation demand. Based on my growth estimates for the company and its present valuation, the stock price is likely to be approximately equal to the present levels in a year.

Operational Analysis

Globant is a technology services provider focusing on a range of key areas, including custom software development and IT services, digital and interactive experiences, cloud services, artificial intelligence and data analytics, and industry-specific expertise.

The company's AI Reinvention Network is particularly noteworthy as a core future growth driver-it is designed to accelerate AI adoption across various industries, including automotive, healthcare, finance, and more. Moreover, the company has introduced AI Agents into the software development lifecycle, which enhances productivity by automating tasks throughout the development process.

I think management is correct in focusing on AI as one of its core competencies-the AI market is likely to be one of the largest in the world, based on my analysis. Globant has significantly increased its AI talent pool by hiring data scientists and AI engineers, and I expect that as the company continues to consolidate its talent and position here, it will continue to prove highly accretive to the company.

Globant's focus on digital transformation services means it is very well-positioned as trends in digital modernization continue to expand. The company has also developed a very strong expertise in cloud migration and management, allowing it to capitalize on the current growth opportunity in cloud services. Moreover, management has established partnerships with Big Tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), allowing Globant to integrate cutting-edge technologies into its solutions, making its digital transformation services far more comprehensive.

There's a lot to be bullish on here; however, as with any high-growth industry, the digital transformation market is highly saturated. That being said, it is also likely big enough to support many larger and smaller players for the foreseeable future.

In terms of direct competitors of a similar size, Globant competes with Endava (DAVA), EPAM Systems (EPAM), and ASGN Incorporated (ASGN). Of course, these are not the only companies that Globant faces competition risk from; it also competes with the much more dominant Accenture (ACN), Infosys (INFY), and Cognizant (CTSH).

Valuation & Financial Analysis

Globant is currently the most highly valued company based on EV to EBITDA of its similar-sized peers that I have identified. However, it is also deservedly so based on its markedly higher EBITDA growth. The company's EBITDA margin of 15% is over 50% higher than the sector median, so I think management should really be commended for successful profitability at the operational level. That being said, its gross margin is 28.3% lower than the sector median. Therefore, if management can find ways to reduce the cost of goods sold, it could become dominantly stronger than its similar-sized peers (it currently has the highest gross margin already). Globant also currently has a higher gross margin by approximately three percentage points than Accenture.

The company's next earnings results are on 15 November, and the consensus estimate indicates a 9.76% year-over-year growth in its normalized EPS. Moreover, the consensus revenue estimate is $614.58 million, which is a quarter-over-quarter growth of 4.6% from Q2's revenue of $587.46 million.

Given that the company is experiencing strong tailwinds from its AI-related revenues at the moment, I find it highly unlikely that the company will underperform the consensus estimates, which are approximately in the middle of management's guidance. Furthermore, the company utilizes a nearshore development model through its Latin American offices, offering cost-effective services while maintaining cultural and time zone compatibility with North American clients; I expect this is likely to substantially support the company's growth in 2025, which is likely to be a stronger year due to lower interest rates supporting digital transformation spending.

Given my outlook on the company's strong operational positioning at present, I estimate that the company will achieve a normalized EPS of $1.64 and revenue of $615 million for the quarter.

However, with the company's forward P/E non-GAAP ratio currently a 29% contraction from its five-year average but its forward diluted EPS growth a 45% contraction from its five-year average, I think there is some valid cause for concern here from an earning-based valuation standpoint. This weakness in its valuation is further reinforced by the fact that it has a forward P/S ratio that is a 36% contraction from its five-year average but forward revenue growth that is a 42% contraction from its five-year average.

Based on this, I estimate that the company's P/E non-GAAP ratio will contract over the next 12 months-I find it unlikely that the stock's valuation multiples will decline in direct correlation to its financial growth rate contractions because I think the market holds strong sentiment for this company's future prospects, particularly related to its position in AI. Therefore, in a base case, a P/E non-GAAP ratio contraction to 30 over the next 12 months seems reasonable to me. I estimate that the company will achieve approximately a 16.5% normalized EPS growth rate for FY25, arriving at a normalized EPS of approximately $7.46 for the full year. Therefore, my 12-month price target for the stock is approximately $223.80. This is equal to the current price. Therefore, my rating is a Hold.

Risk Analysis

Especially at the moment with heightened AI demand, Globant's revenue is sensitive to fluctuations in client spending. I expect that as major AI companies finalize their initial AI infrastructure build-outs, the initial demand for AI services will begin to slow down following this. There is the possibility that we will see a contraction in revenues for many companies that initially benefitted from the high initial demand for AI. The current high demand for AI is not something I expect to stay constant (although I do expect it to scale in cycles over time as new technology proficiencies and macroeconomic conditions facilitate this). Therefore, I think any company investing heavily in generating revenue from AI demand should expect a period of protracted decline (unless revenue is sourced as recurring, but even then, one-off services will likely reduce significantly). To put it more simply, I find AI spending to be highly susceptible to recession.

Moreover, while AI and robotics markets have a very strong overlap, there are distinct differences in the expertise of talent required in both fields. Therefore, without proper foresight and preparation, management could easily fail to develop the right employee base to secure high-revenue contracts in robotics transformations. Management will want to be strategic in its timing here because too heavy an emphasis on robotics too early on will be negative and impact the company's margins significantly.

In keeping with the above chart, management needs to anticipate when we are near a major upcycle in robotics to prepare its infrastructure in advance. Another way management could do this (and I believe it has shown evidence of this strategy already with its emphasis on AI) is to build an agile culture with the necessary cohorts that can then pivot to robotics quickly when the demand picks up-predicting a major upcycle is incredibly difficult.

Conclusion

I am bullish on Globant operationally, but the reality is that not every great business can deliver alpha. Therefore, my rating must be a Hold, even though I admire management and the positioning of its company cost-effectively in a very high-growth field. I expect that with the right preparedness, the company will continue to capitalize on trends in modern digitalization for years to come. However, at its current valuation, investors would be better off looking elsewhere for high returns, especially as Globant does not pay a dividend at this time.

data.title: NLR: Nuclear Boom Doesn't Trickle Down Much To Utility Shareholders
data.description:
The VanEck Uranium and Nuclear Energy ETF (NYSEARCA:NLR) is a pretty broad utilities ETF focused on companies with significant generation from nuclear capacity, but also some stocks with positive exposure to Uranium prices.

Nuclear in general has seen a bit of a revival, according to our broader thesis on the theme some years ago, with the added (debatable) benefit of the AI revolution further amping demand with nice economics from long-dated PPA agreements on the very high operating leverage nuclear assets across the industry. While nuclear generators benefit somewhat from PPA wins which is nice and may justify keeping these assets in use longer, there are still mitigating factors in that they are expensive to maintain beyond their intended life even if there are maybe some discounting effect benefits from doing so and of course the opportunity to earn continued intermediate income without needing to start a new generation project. The dwindled human capital and expertise in developing and maintaining nuclear assets hasn't helped either, with several generations having come into a world where it was being assumed that nuclear was on the way out.

Nuclear is great, and altogether has been an underutilised quantum-leap technology produced by a rare gathering of the Western geniuses of the 20th century. However, we don't generally see much incremental upside from the recent headlines for utilities stocks in connection with AI training power. PPA agreements are typically long-dated so there's nothing special about the length of these agreements, and while AI is a secular demand sink for power, electricity prices aren't going off the charts on AI's account, although they are generally higher now than 5 years ago.

In all, a market to develop nuclear assets doesn't necessarily mean outstanding incremental returns for the utility companies which are saddled with big responsibilities in generating nuclear energy and are by regulatory design not set up for extraordinary returns. In fact, IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) reported negative NPVs for developing and only marginally better but still negative NPVs for extended-life models, from before the costs of capital rose after 2019. We don't really see obvious value creation for the nuclear utilities other than the markets maybe having more of a taste for nuclear exposure now than they used to, meaning less idiosyncratic discounts, and in limited cases where restarts are happening of nuclear plants. But this is speculative. As such, while small-scale reactor companies like Oklo (OKLO) and particularly modular reactor stocks might be trendy and interesting right now, and maybe stocks with more explicit Uranium spot price exposure as Uranium demand should be growing, we aren't particularly moved to go for NLR just because nuclear is less taboo now, and would probably make a more specific bet within nuclear and not on the utilities.

NLR Quick Breakdown

The NLR portfolio is composed substantially of straightforward utility companies in the US, some spun off from larger ones, containing a nuclear skew in their generating portfolios. These are utility companies, so the name of the game is the same as any other utility which is purchase price agreement wins to keep assets properly utilised and the hope of high electricity prices (for those that don't do CCGT). This is how all utility companies basically function, either establishing PPAs for assets in development (to finance asset development securely) or rolling over PPAs to keep current generating assets highly utilised. These agreements are often with corporate customers depending on the structure of the electricity markets.

The big headlines lately have been that Google (GOOG) has entered into agreements with small-scale reactor producers in order to deal with the power demand for training AI at their data centers. We understand why the small-scale reactor class of nuclear stocks would go up on account of new and important business, but ETFs like the NLR have also gone up significantly. There have been other headlines surging utility companies in any instance where they are named as having signed PPA agreements with AI-oriented companies.

Considerations

We have some reasons to think the run-up of NLR may not be entirely sensible. Firstly, some utility companies have increased on announcements of AI-related PPA agreements. Constellation (CEG) energy has picked up meaningfully. Electricity prices haven't made any meaningful movements sequentially, but the announcement that Microsoft (MSFT) has entered into a 20-year PPA CEG caused some stock action. In the case of Constellation Energy, the excitement might be coming from the fact that the nuclear assets in question are going to be restarted after being put down before COVID-19, and that Microsoft might be paying a premium in the PPA. However, that same asset was shut down for economic reasons in 2019, reflecting the challenges in these assets. Electricity prices are quite a bit higher than they were in 2019, so it is possibly a positive outcome ultimately as that asset returns to profits. Also, Cameco (CCJ) within NLR is restarting some of its Uranium operations that were also paused due to a weak Uranium market, but they're not a utility, although it's a positive revision for the company. However, going back to the nuclear generating companies, not that many restarts are in store most likely and consistent with that investors should generally keep the following in mind:

  • PPA agreements of 20-year length are entirely within the norm, so there shouldn't be a big incremental change in the certainty profile of the PPA portfolio at the company from getting a new deal like that.
  • Demand for nuclear specifically as a green and high-capacity factor energy source may mean that nuclear assets slated for decommissioning may not be decommissioned on their original schedule, and their lives instead extended. Many assets have received extensions in the name of energy security post Ukraine war. However, this should not necessarily mean much incremental benefit for the company's shareholders that steward these assets. Firstly, speculation around these issues tend to not impact nuclear-levered utility stocks in Europe, which is usually a more rational and less algorithmically driven market due to non-uniform disclosure systems compared to the US where everything ends up on SEC.gov. That is an important sign that the economic benefits are debatable.
  • Secondly, this is consistent with the cost to extend the life of a nuclear asset by 10 years which tends to be as much as the decommissioning cost (both in the range of $500-800 million). Yes, staving off decommissioning comes with some positive discounting effects, and the asset continues to generate income so it has a positive incremental NPV effect, but it is still a major investment to keep nuclear running especially as talent for developing and maintaining nuclear, as well as component supply, has been underinvested for some decades now. Also, a lot of the life extension investment is upfront. We think a positive thing could be the longer the life of the asset the more chance for uprate investments, but these investments are probably expensive enough too where the incremental shareholder value creation from making them is not enough to justify price action, and the uprate matter doesn't appear to be part of the established narrative.

Conclusions

We like nuclear, and think utilising it is probably a good idea since it's already there. But for the utility company business model, even those with more nuclear skew, we don't see anything in the headlines around AI PPA agreements that would uniquely benefit them over PPA agreements with other commercial customers. As said, we also think that it is highly debatable whether life extensions of nuclear assets actually benefits the shareholders of the utility companies that have to steward these assets. New asset development would be even more questionable, although no one is talking about that yet. In the case of a restart, this might be a positive revision, but could still mean negative incremental NPVs as decommissioning costs are inevitable. Also, opportunities for worthwhile asset restarts are unclear and likely limited. While electricity prices are generally higher than they were at the time of these reports in 2019, so are capital costs. LCOEs, or the net present cost of generating electricity used for comparing generation methods, were barely changing in the extended models.

Also, if nuclear assets continue on much longer than expected, this might also impact electricity prices negatively as the expected amount of future supply is revised upwards in negotiations for PPAs. Perhaps the nuclear discount is shrinking, and to the extent that NLR is exposed to Uranium spot prices the decent performance there would also help NLR performance, but other than that and maybe the more particular instances like CEG which is restarting a mothballed asset, we wouldn't expect that a nuclear boom would be especially good for shareholders, and we aren't convinced that it explains or justifies the NLR's 42% 1-year growth which comes down to performance from other factors in the NLR's relatively concentrated portfolio. We wouldn't make a utility play on nuclear so NLR isn't our favourite.

data.title: AbbVie: Strong Dividends But Humira Disappoints
data.description:
Healthcare is both defensive and a mean reversion case

After writing a recent article on Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), I had some requests in the comment section to cover AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV). This is a stock I have owned for a while that was purchased a few years ago as part of a "Magic Formula" 30 stock buy and hold strategy that I implement for a part of my portfolio.

The company remains on the Magic Formula screener as a good value, starting at stocks with a market cap of $80 billion or larger. However, the screener is not flawless and there are some anomalies that make free cash flow larger than the valuation metric I used, "owner earnings". The main difference is the line item "write down and restructuring costs" which was recorded on the cash flow statement at nearly $5 billion on a trailing twelve-month basis is not a sustainable add-back to cash from operations. Owner earnings only utilize Depreciation and Amortization as an add-back.

AbbVie is a decent deal based on the dividend, but new CEO Robert Michael who took over for Richard Gonzalez, an internal promotion from Michael's COO role prior to Gonzalez's retirement, made it seem like single-digit growth is about the best the company has in store for the next few years. More in a bit, but the Humira patent expiration in 2023 also presents a bigger problem than I think the new CEO's first earnings call opined. I own the stock and will continue to, but the stock is currently a hold in my opinion until either the stock grows into its valuation or the price comes down.

Current story and trends

AbbVie is one of the pure pharma plays in healthcare that is solely reliant on its drug sales and in-organic acquisitions of other drug companies to grow top and bottom lines. They are dissimilar to a company like Johnson & Johnson, which also has a med tech division and is more in line with competitors like Amgen (AMGN) or Pfizer (PFE).

The main area where AbbVie differs from other pharmaceutical companies is that they have one of the highest exposures to "immunology" versus oncology drugs, at more than 50% revenue exposure. Think of things like psoriasis, Crohn's disease and other drugs related to autoimmune disorders.

Humira exclusivity has ended

An excellent 2023 piece from Healio describes just how big of a deal Humira is:

In 2021, Humira — the blockbuster biologic that has for years been the highest grossing drug in the world — accomplished something that no drug had previously achieved when its global revenues topped $20 billion.

Through both biosimilar competition and an ending of exclusivity in 2023, the Humira boom is over. Revenues have started to deteriorate, and the ending of this blockbuster drug's exclusivity was probably a factor in the previous CEO stepping down at the top of the mountain. It didn't get much better for the company than the 10-year run up to 2023 which saw revenues absolutely boom.

Both revenue and gross profit more than doubled during these 10 years, and the new CEO has big shoes to fill to keep investors happy for the next 10.

Revenue exposure

All numbers in millions courtesy of ABBVIE most recent 10-Q:

SEGMENT REVENUE PERCENT
Immunology 6971 56.5%
Oncology 1506 12.2%
Aesthetics 1390 11.27%
Neuroscience 1931 15.65%
Eye care 533 4.32%
Total from major segments 12331

This is the most recent revenue exposure for AbbVie from their most recent quarter's 10-Q covering not all of their revenue, but the revenue that covers their wholly owned drugs. We can see that AbbVie is a heavy play on immunology. I personally think this is going to be a growing industry as someone who suffers from food allergies. The amount of specialized foods that now exist to help allergy sufferers with autoimmune disorders is a great indication that this trajectory is only going one way.

With Rheumatoid Arthritis being one of the main presenting issues from autoimmune disorders along with intestinal and dermatological, Humira was definitely a "catch-all" for this segment.

IMMUNOLOGY SEGMENT Revenue Percent of segment
Humira 2814 41.20%
Skyrizi 2727 40.90%
Rinvoq 1430 17.70%
total 6971

With the self-proclaimed "degradation" of Humira earnings on the most recent quarter's earnings call, Skyrizi is coming up the back end in the immunology segment and will soon become AbbVie's leading drug. This drug goes beyond mere Rheumatoid Arthritis and gets into being a cytokine disruptor aimed at alleviating a whole host of autoimmune issues including plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's Disease, and Ulcerative Colitis.

With the primary patent for Skyrizi not expiring until 2033, this will be the primary cash cow for the company until further notice, in my opinion.

Upcoming Earnings

With earnings set to report October 30th, there have been 10 upward revisions since the last earnings report, where the new CEO guided higher on the call. I did find it odd that he expressed such confidence in how well everything was going after a miss on the bottom line.

Keep in mind, in comparison to last year, Humira revenue has declined 31.6% on a 3-month basis and 35.4% on a 6-month basis. If this keeps up and other drugs don't pick up the slack, it will be difficult to keep growing at high single digits as guided.

The company has already started to look at earnings growth "ex Humira", which means they are putting that top revenue generator on a perpetual negative growth train to hades.

Measuring reflexive trends

10-year trend

On a ten-year basis, the reflexive trends for long-term holders have not been horrible, with free cash flow growing well ahead of price growth. However, we see obvious trends in chart lines dipping downward starting in 2023 for revenue, normalized EPS, and free cash flow. Let's zoom in closer at the 5-year level.

5-year trend

On a 5-year comparison, we now see all major profitability metrics [revenue, free cash flow and normalized EPS] dipping well below price growth during this period. For new buyers, the current trend might not be your friend.

Valuation

AbbVie is a slow grower. Thus, this company needs to be weighed against its current "cheapness" using the risk-free rate.

In this model, I use the classic Warren Buffett "Owner Earnings" model. Similar to discounted cash flow, except this takes into account taxes, interest and stock-based compensation. This model uses GAAP net income as it's base profitability metric and then adds back depreciation and amortization. Finally, TTM CAPEX is deducted.

This is all discounted by the risk-free rate using the 10-year Treasury. Buffett argued that if a company could predictably grow earnings from the point of entry into the future, then an investment in the evaluated stock would be better than an investment in a risk-free alternative like the 10-year Treasury since that is a fixed return versus a growing one for said company. Currently, the risk-free rate sits around 4.2%, and I will use this as my discount rate.

In the case of AbbVie on a trailing twelve-month basis, we have the following:

All numbers in millions courtesy of Seeking Alpha:

  • Net Income: $5,339
  • Depreciation and Amortization: $8,516
  • TTM CAPEX:$858
  • Equation: [$5,339 + $8,516]-$858= $12,997 "Owner Earnings"
  • $12,997/4.2%= $309,452 fair market cap.
  • Divided by shares outstanding [1,766.3] = $175.19 fair price.

With consensus estimated earnings guidance slated at 11% growth in EPS from FY 2024 to FY 2025, that would put the fair price at $194.46 if all other items in this model remained constant [CAPEX requirements and depreciation and amortization]. That would make AbbVie slightly undervalued based on next year's earnings, but I'd rather see the profitability trend lines tick positive before I take the chance to buy more shares with such a thin margin of safety.

The dividend

As a percentage of free cash flow per share:

  • TTM FCF/share= $10.05
  • FCF payout ratio= 61%

This is one of the main items that makes this stock popular, it pays a really nice dividend with growth for 11 years and a moderate payout ratio.

The dividend is actually second best on this list of peers in my opinion as AbbVie provides a 3.28% yield which is still covered on a GAAP earnings basis. Gilead Sciences (GILD) would be the top yield at 3.55% and still covered.

Even though the dividend looks safe for now, they are going through a write-down phase with Humira that is freeing up some free cash flow with the write-offs but creating more moving parts, making it more difficult to predict future free cash flow.

Balance sheet

This balance sheet is a bit of a mixed bag observing my 3 favorite metrics, shares outstanding, long-term debt and cash and short-term investments.

Share count may tend to fluctuate in big pharma names as acquisitions come into the pipeline, which may be purchased with shares if advantageous due to share price appreciation.

Long-term debt has dipped a bit and cash has leveled out since those share issuances. We can clearly see the correlation between cash and shares during that early 2020s period.

Nothing to write home about here, not a particularly strong or weak balance sheet, with LT debt about 5 X cash and only about 2 turns greater than the company's $26.12 Billion TTM EBITDA.

Share buybacks would be welcome here to start getting the float back on a downward trajectory, or else AbbVie might start creating its own headwinds to beating analyst expectations for EPS.

Catalysts

Cerevel was the most recent big acquisition closing in August, here are some details for the bolt on outlook:

AbbVie's acquisition of Cerevel strengthens our foundation in neuroscience and positions us to deliver sustainable long-term performance into the next decade and beyond," said Robert A. Michael, chief executive officer, AbbVie. "Our new Cerevel colleagues share our commitment to deliver meaningful change for patients living with neurological and psychiatric conditions. We are excited to welcome the talented Cerevel team to AbbVie."

There are multiple programs in Cerevel's pipeline across several neurological and psychiatric conditions such as schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease and mood disorders, where there continues to be significant unmet need for patients. Cerevel's pipeline is highly complementary to AbbVie's existing neuroscience portfolio and the completion of the acquisition is an important step forward to delivering new and better tolerated therapies.

Keep in mind, Cerevel is a pre-revenue mid-stage drug developer that AbbVie can now take to market, but the end market is in no way a proven top or bottom line grower.

In Cerevel's most recent 10Q, they lay out the R&D costs for their drug lineup, listed in thousands. This is the final 10Q before going private and becoming part of AbbVie.

The total R&D cash burn before absorption stood at over $100 million.

The other growth areas, as focused on by CEO Robert Michael, were the cross-selling of the different immunology drugs. From my understanding, many of these immunology drugs can be prescribed for different autoimmune disorders. Having the drugs prescribed for new autoimmune disorders that they were not previously prescribed for are new organic growth areas.

Risks and summary

We have a new CEO after an amazing run with Humira, and a doubling in top-line revenue over the last decade.

Now the company seems to be relying on a bump in other immunology drugs entering new autoimmune prescriptions combined with a growth bump in oncology and growth in neuroscience. The company has goals and a clear trajectory of what they want to do, but it could be a long road and the current growth rate lines in all profitability metrics are pointing in the wrong direction.

The stock also seems overvalued on an owner-earnings model up against this year's earnings and moderately undervalued up against next year's earnings if they hit analysts' predicted growth rates. This is a hold for the nice covered dividend, but not cheap enough for a buy rating based on profitability trends.
