data.title: Tackle Fear And Uncertainties With Big Recurrent Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

It's that time of the year again. Halloween decorations are being put up, movie trailers for scary movies are being released, and marathons of old scary movies are airing on TV. Some people love the feeling of being scared, and October provides us with many options to seek that feeling. You can watch a scary movie, go on a haunted hay ride, visit haunted houses, or get lost in a corn maze.

One place none of us wants to be scared is our portfolios. Yet, in recent weeks, we've been getting an increase in questions within our Investing Group and comments on our public articles–questions that tell me people are scared.

  • What about the ILA port strike?
  • Will war escalate in Israel?
  • What about Hurricane Milton?
  • Who will win the Presidential race? People might not agree which outcome is scary, but many people believe that one of the outcomes is.
  • What about a recession?
  • What about a Black Swan?

Based on the questions we've been receiving, people are scared. Their first reaction?

Fear Is Natural

First, let me be clear: if you have these types of fears, there is absolutely nothing wrong with that. Fear is a natural part of investing. After all, we are managing portfolios that represent a substantial portion of our net worth.

It doesn't matter whether you have thousands, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands, or millions; we all share something in common. The amounts in our portfolios represent a very substantial portion of our life savings. It is an amount we sacrificed to build up for years, passing on all the various things we could have spent that money on. It is very likely that during your working years, your peers were living grander lifestyles. They were living in nicer houses, driving nicer cars, taking more lavish vacations, etc. Many who earned a similar income to yourself, chose to spend instead of save. You reading this article tells me that whatever your income was, you decided not to spend it all. You decided to invest in your future, and that is a sacrifice.

So, when you are looking at your nest egg that you worked so hard to build, it is perfectly natural to fear that something might happen to it. There is absolutely nothing wrong with having these emotions. However, we do need to identify when these emotions might be having an adverse impact on our decisions. For example, a parent is going to be a bit fearful the first time their 16-year-old drives off in the car alone, but it wouldn't be healthy if they decided that their child should never learn how to drive to avoid that feeling.

Uncertainty Is Inevitable

As investors, we frequently face the reality that we don't know everything. Publicly traded companies report earnings once every three months. While we might get glimpses of information through various press releases or news reports, this information is frequently incomplete. We only get the full picture when the company files a 10-K or 10-Q. As a result, we have to fill in the gaps with estimations and educated guesses.

It is obvious that we don't know the future, but it is also important to keep in mind that we often don't have perfect information about what is happening right now.

All of your investment decisions will be made with a certain degree of uncertainty.

Doubt Is Healthy, To A Point

When you mix fear with the uncertainty that is inherent in investing, you get doubt. You start second-guessing whether you made the right investments. You will start to doubt whether you made a smart decision by deciding to buy or sell.

This is actually very healthy. Every once in a while, it is good to go through your portfolio and remind yourself why you bought what you did. This is one reason why we make it a point to routinely cover earnings. We want to take a fresh look at the stocks we own and decide if they are still good investments.

You should be checking in on your portfolio holdings occasionally, to see if things are going as you expect and consider making changes if it is appropriate.

On the other hand, if you are making frequent adjustments in your portfolio, buying, and selling the same stock multiple times, that might indicate an unhealthy level of doubt. When you make an investment, it isn't realistic to expect that the price is only going to go up. Stock prices are volatile. When we invest in publicly traded stocks, we should expect prices to be volatile. We shouldn't worry about it because it is natural and expected.

The Income Method Helps Us Separate the Noise From the Risk

One of the reasons we created the Income Method is that it helps us distinguish between events that we should react to and events that we don't need to react to. The Income Method emphasizes focusing on the income an investment is producing, rather than the share price.

This is a key point because there are many news items that might impact an investment's trading price but are not likely to threaten its income. Some people misinterpret our focus on income as an assertion that income never changes. This is false.

Dividends do change. Our portfolio has dividend raises and cuts every single year. Companies default on interest payments. Corporate bankruptcies happen every year. When you follow the Income Method and have a portfolio of 42+ income-producing investments, which we call The Rule of 42, you will see your income change every quarter. You will see dividend raises and cuts every single year.

Focusing on income helps us differentiate between issues that have a real impact on the bottom line and profitability of an investment and issues that are temporary and won't have a long-term impact on an investment.

When faced with a news item like a hurricane that causes substantial property damage, we can turn on the news and see the most devastating imagery. We can empathize with those whose lives are being dramatically disrupted and sadly lost in some cases.

While you're watching the news, you will eventually start to wonder, "What does this mean for my investments?"

Realty Income (O) owns 725 properties accounting for 3.6% of annual rent in Georgia, 493 properties in North Carolina, and 570 in Tennessee. In short, O likely owns over 1,000 properties that experienced some degree of impact from Hurricane Helene. Most REITs own many properties in the areas that were hit, as the "Sun Belt" has been a region highly favored by REITs due to its growth outlook.

What does this mean for O? It isn't like this is O's first hurricane. It has gone through Hurricane Katrina, Ian, Michael, Andrew, and numerous smaller storms and none of them interrupted the dividend payment for even a month.

The damage is real, the toll on the people impacted is certainly tragic and if you dive deep enough, you can calculate the financial impact. Yet, the reality is that for most publicly traded companies, the financial impact isn't large enough to materially alter their earnings. At the end of the day, earnings are what pay interest and dividends.

You will find that with many of these events, the financial impact is nowhere close to large enough to plausibly put the dividend at risk. When that is the case, income investors can simply look at any trading-induced dip as a buying opportunity.

Occasionally, events are large enough to put a dividend at risk. For example, Hawaiian Electric Industries (HE) suspended its dividend following the wildfires in Maui, as it faced substantial cap-ex needs and potential liability for numerous lawsuits.

Identifying When To Dig Deeper

As income investors, our focus needs to be to estimate the impact that a particular event will have on earnings and determine if that puts the dividend at risk. If it does, that is when we want to consider our future with that investment. If it doesn't, we can comfortably hold through whatever price volatility the market produces.

Instead of asking whether an event is "good" or "bad" for a company, ask yourself, "Does this event put earnings under significant enough pressure to put the dividend at risk?" Losing buildings to a hurricane or flooding is never a good thing. It is a cost to REITs like O, and that cost is primarily reflected in insurance costs, as these buildings are always insured.

If you really want to grow a pointy head and bury yourself in spreadsheets, I'm sure you could come to a rough dollar figure for how much Hurricane Helene will impact REITs through higher insurance costs in the future. Yet, at the end of the day, you would be putting in a lot of effort for a result that doesn't mean much. Your analysis can begin and end with, "is the damage from Hurricane Helene significant enough to have a material impact on earnings and threaten the dividend?" In this case, No, it isn't.

So often, I see investors go down the rabbit hole trying to calculate the precise impact of something that can clearly be dismissed as immaterial on its face. Attempting to account for every penny across the 42+ companies you invest in is something that is neither possible nor practical. Fortunately, it isn't necessary. By focusing on the income, we can bring ourselves back to the surface so that we don't get lost in the minutiae.

With each issue, we can ask ourselves whether it is plausible that a particular issue might threaten the dividend. If the answer is "maybe," that is when we want to put effort into digging deeper and doing calculations. We can keep going until either the answer is "no, this won't impact the dividend", or until we decide it is too uncertain, and we can get safer income elsewhere.

A lot of things will happen that are "bad" for a particular company. Every company in the world is always facing challenges that they have to overcome. That is simply part of running a business. Those of you who have run your own businesses are familiar with the never-ending list of "problems" that you had to solve. As fast as you solve one, three more crop up. By focusing on the income, we can take a step back and only spend our valuable time on the problems that are potentially big enough to impact our bottom line.

Bear Markets & Recession

If you are part of our investing group, you know I'm in the camp that believes a recession is likely coming soon and that will almost certainly mean a "bear market". Some have suggested that the upcoming recession might be among the most severe we have ever seen in the United States. I disagree, I think the upcoming recession is most likely to be quite mild from a fundamental standpoint. However, life is uncertain, and I could certainly be wrong.

What if those who believe that we might see a bear market that lasts 10+ years are right? Does that change my investment strategy? Should I run to cash and sit on my piles of cash, waiting for Armageddon?

No.

We invest in companies that produce cash flow and pay that cash flow out to investors in the form of interest and dividends. Is that cash flow guaranteed? No. It isn't guaranteed in any environment; as noted above, we see changes in our portfolio every single year. During a black swan, we will see more dividend cuts than usual, and more companies than usual will default on their interest payments.

We saw this during COVID-19, which was far worse than most recessions are on dividend payments. Companies that had perfectly "safe" dividends in January 2020 were cutting/suspending their dividends in March. Did it nullify the Income Method? No.

Members who followed our plan found that their income had substantially, if not fully, recovered by the end of the year. Through 2021 and 2022, they saw their income growing at a much faster pace than usual. The reason is that even in events that influence almost everything, not every company is equally impacted. Everyone in the world was impacted by the pandemic, but even during COVID-19, many companies continued raising their dividends. Investors who focused on the income were able to find opportunities to sell those who were suspending their dividends and reinvest into investments that had much safer income.

Our investment strategy accounts for the risk of periods with more dividend cuts than usual. The Income Method has several protective measures.

  1. Diversification. We follow The Rule of 42, which emphasizes owning at least 42 different income-producing investments. Even during periods of elevated dividend cuts, not every investment in every sector is going to be cut at the same time. Companies don't cut dividends because a recession or bear market is happening. Companies cut dividends because their earnings are expected to decline to a level where management believes paying the current dividend is unwise. In every recession, there are always certain segments of the economy that bear the brunt of the impact and other segments that aren't hit so hard. A recession is not uniform throughout the economy, and frequently, the sectors hit worst in the prior recession are not the same as those that will be impacted the most in the next one. By being diversified, we can ensure that only a portion of our portfolio is impacted significantly enough that dividends are cut.
  2. A high allocation to fixed-income. Even in the segments that are hit the hardest by recessions, fixed-income investments are more stable. Common dividends are entirely voluntary and are the first to be reduced or eliminated. Preferred dividends are pre-determined and companies are only going to suspend them when the issues are very serious. Interest payments are required, and if a company fails to pay them, they are going to go bankrupt more often than not. As a result, a portfolio of fixed income is likely to see much more stable income even in a very severe recession.
  3. Reinvesting at least 25% of your income. A large part of the Income Method is to ensure that you have a cushion. Based on our experience and backtesting, planning on reinvesting 25% is enough to ensure that your withdrawals will remain covered even through periods where dividend cuts are high.

These guidelines will help ensure that you have cash flowing into your brokerage even during the worst periods. What happens when you have cash flowing into your brokerage when prices are super low because the market is in a panic? It means you can buy opportunities that are producing cash flow at much higher than normal yields.

"Focus on the income" is not a statement that says, ignore reality and just buy anything that currently has cash flow. It means that we focus on buying companies with a lot of cash flow and are able and willing to pass that cash flow along to investors. We don't panic over oscillations in market prices. We aren't trying to predict world events and trying to trade ahead of the crowd. We aren't trying to duck in and out of the market.

Instead, we look at each company, understand the business model, and do our best to predict whether that company will be able to continue paying us a generous dividend. Sometimes we will be wrong, that's investing. However, the guidelines outlined in the Income Method exist specifically to protect us from the reality that we will be wrong, some events will happen that we fail to predict, and some investments won't provide the cash flow we expected. That's ok. When you have a good, robust strategy, your portfolio can absorb mistakes, it can absorb you being wrong sometimes, and it can absorb recessions.

If your strategy relies on you always being right, on you being able to predict when prices will change, when bear markets will start, when the bottom is in, and when the next Black Swan is going to swim by – I wish you the best of luck. My strategy doesn't require me to be right all the time, which is a good thing because I'm not going to be right all the time.

When you reach the point where you have the confidence that your investment strategy is solid and that it can absorb your mistakes and the unforeseen, you will find yourself having a lot less fear, uncertainty, or doubt. While you might second guess specific positions, you will have the confidence that your overall portfolio is capable of surviving anything and that the strategy you have implemented will lead you to success.

The Income Method is that strategy for me and thousands of my closest friends.
